definition: **Merging** combines two branches by creating a new *merge commit* with both branches as parents — preserving the actual history of when work diverged and reconverged. **Rebasing** moves the commits of one branch onto a different base, *rewriting* them as if they were authored on top of the new base — producing a clean linear history at the cost of altering commit identity.
layman: Imagine two diary entries written in parallel notebooks. **Merging** is taping both notebooks together so you can see they happened in parallel. **Rebasing** is re-copying one notebook so it reads as if it happened *after* the other, in a single straight line.
content:
## Merging and rebasing

These are the two ways to combine work from different branches. They produce the same *content* but very different *history*.

### Merge — preserves history, creates a merge commit

A merge combines two branches by creating a new commit with **two parents**:

```
    A---B---C    feature
   /         \
--D---E---F---M  main   (M is the merge commit)
```

- The merge commit `M` has two parents: the tip of `main` (F) and the tip of `feature` (C).
- Both branches' commits remain unchanged.
- History shows the actual story: `feature` branched at D, did A/B/C in parallel with E/F, and was merged back.

#### Fast-forward merge

If the target branch hasn't moved since you branched off, Git can just slide the branch pointer forward instead of making a merge commit:

```
Before:  --D---E   main
              \
               A---B   feature

After fast-forward:  --D---E---A---B   main, feature
```

No merge commit, no diverging history. Use `git merge --no-ff` if you *want* the merge commit even when fast-forward is possible (useful for marking 'this is when feature X landed').

### Rebase — rewrites history, no merge commit

A rebase takes the commits unique to your branch and *replays* them on top of a new base. Each commit gets a new SHA because its parent changes.

```
Before:
    A---B---C    feature
   /
--D---E---F     main

After `git rebase main` (on feature):
            A'--B'--C'   feature
           /
--D---E---F             main
```

A, B, C still represent the same *changes* — but they're now A', B', C' with new SHAs because each one's parent shifted.

Rebasing produces a **linear history** that reads like a single straight line of development. No octopus diagrams in `git log`.

### Interactive rebase — `git rebase -i`

Interactive rebase lets you edit, reorder, squash or drop your commits before sharing them. Common uses:

- **Squash** five 'fix typo' commits into one clean commit.
- **Reword** an unhelpful commit message.
- **Reorder** commits into a logical sequence.
- **Drop** an experimental commit that didn't make the cut.

```bash
git rebase -i HEAD~5    # opens an editor with the last 5 commits
```

### The golden rule of rebase

**Never rebase commits you've pushed to a shared branch.** Rebase rewrites SHAs; anyone who pulled the old commits now has duplicates and broken history. Rebase your *private* branch onto the latest `main` all you want; rebase a *shared* branch only with team consensus.

### When to use which

| Scenario | Merge | Rebase |
|----------|-------|--------|
| Pulling latest `main` into your feature branch | acceptable | preferred (keeps your branch linear) |
| Long-lived release branch | preferred (preserves history) | risky |
| Bringing a feature into `main` | both work — team chooses by policy | both work — team chooses by policy |
| Cleaning up your local commits before PR | n/a | preferred (interactive squash) |
| Resolving a conflict on a shared branch | preferred (one merge commit) | painful (re-resolve on every commit) |

### Why this matters for Gen AI

- **Eval reproducibility** — a linear, rebased history makes `git bisect` lightning fast for 'which commit broke the eval?'.
- **Reviewable PRs** — interactive rebase lets you present 5 logical commits instead of 25 'wip' commits, dramatically speeding reviews.
- **Audit trail** — when a model regression hits prod, clean history makes 'who changed this and why' answerable in seconds.
- **Branching for experiments** — rebasing a long-running experiment onto fresh `main` keeps it integrable; merging keeps the *story* of when each experiment happened.
keyPoints: Merge creates a new commit with two parents — preserves history.
Rebase replays your commits onto a new base — produces linear history but rewrites SHAs.
Fast-forward is a degenerate merge where Git just moves the branch pointer; no merge commit.
Interactive rebase (`git rebase -i`) is the standard tool for cleaning up local commits before a PR.
Golden rule: never rebase commits that have been shared on a public branch.
